title: PS10: Geospatial Data
text:
NoteGeneral Instructions




Download the template file ps10.qmd to answer the questions.
Rename ps10.qmd to ps10-first-last.qmd, where first and last are your first and last names (e.g. ps10-gaston-sanchez.qmd).
Download the file countypres_2000-2020.csv from bcourses and put it in the same directory as your qmd file. It is present in the description of this assignment and under files/problem-sets/ps10/
Run install.packages('usmap') in the R console. You will need this package for a section of the assignment. Do not put this in a code cell in the assignment.
Render your qmd file to an HTML file and submit both your qmd and HTML files to the corresponding assignment submission in bCourses.

title: PS10: Geospatial Data
text:
1.1 Filtering Data

1.1.1 Combine Date and Time Information
In this dataset, the date and time variables are split into separate columns. Use the make_datetime() function inside mutate to create a new column that is a single datetime object. (Use help(make_datetime) in the R console to learn about this function)

storms = storms |> mutate(datetime = make_datetime(year = year, month = month, day = day, hour = hour))



1.1.2 Find Long Duration Storms
Now, for our purposes, we’re interested in the number of storms that lasted longer than three weeks.

group_by() the name and year columns (this is because names for tropical storms are reused on a six-year rotation, so we have to group by the year column as well to avoid counting different storms as the same),
then use summarize() to get the first() and last() dates of each storm,
subtract these two dates to get the duration of the storm,
then filter to only get storms where the duration is more than 3 weeks.
Save it as a data frame long_storms.


long_storms = storms |> group_by(name, year) |> summarize(start = first(datetime), end = last(datetime)) |> mutate(duration = end - start) |> filter(duration > weeks(3))

`summarise()` has regrouped the output.
ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by name and year.
ℹ Output is grouped by name.
ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(name, year))` for per-operation grouping
  (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.

head(long_storms)

# A tibble: 6 × 5
# Groups:   name [6]
  name      year start               end                 duration    
  <chr>    <dbl> <dttm>              <dttm>              <drtn>      
1 Alberto   2000 2000-08-03 18:00:00 2000-08-25 06:00:00 1857600 secs
2 Ivan      2004 2004-09-02 18:00:00 2004-09-24 06:00:00 1857600 secs
3 Kyle      2002 2002-09-20 18:00:00 2002-10-12 12:00:00 1879200 secs
4 Leslie    2018 2018-09-22 00:00:00 2018-10-14 00:00:00 1900800 secs
5 Nadine    2012 2012-09-10 12:00:00 2012-10-04 06:00:00 2052000 secs
6 Paulette  2020 2020-09-07 00:00:00 2020-09-28 12:00:00 1857600 secs




1.1.3 Merge Two Tables
Now, use a join function to merge long_storms with storms so that we have all of the data in storms and long_storms, but only for the storms that lasted longer than three weeks.

jstorms = storms |> inner_join(long_storms, by = c("name", "year"))

title: PS10: Geospatial Data
text:
1.2 Make Leaflet Maps

1.2.1 Basic Map
Let’s start by making a basic leaflet map by passing the data from the previous part to leaflet, then using addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") and addCircleMarkers. Experiment with the radius, color, stroke, and fillOpacity inputs to addCircleMarkers.

jstorms |> leaflet() |> addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") |> addCircleMarkers(lat = ~lat, lng = ~long, radius = 0.5, color = "red", fillOpacity = 0.4)







1.2.2 Map Including Category Level
Change the radius from a setting to a mapping of the category variable that describes the strength of the storm. In doing so, larger points will match with higher category storms.

jstorms |> leaflet() |> addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") |> addCircleMarkers(lat = ~lat, lng = ~long, radius = ~category*2, color = "red", fillOpacity = 0.4)







1.2.3 Color By Storms
Create a color pallet and replicate the same plot as before, but now color the points based on the name of the storm. Add a legend as well.

pal <- colorFactor(palette = "Set1", domain=jstorms$name)
jstorms |> leaflet() |> addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") |> addCircleMarkers(lat = ~lat, lng = ~long, radius = ~category*2, color = ~pal(name), fillOpacity = 0.4) |> addLegend(pal = pal, values = ~name, title = "name")







1.2.4 Map with Popup
Lastly, copy your code from the previous section and add a popup in addCircleMarkers that shows the datetime of the storm.

pal <- colorFactor(palette = "Set1", domain=jstorms$name)
jstorms |> leaflet() |> addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") |> addCircleMarkers(lat = ~lat, lng = ~long, radius = ~category*2, color = ~pal(name), fillOpacity = 0.4, popup = ~paste0("Storm: ", name, "Category: ", category)) |> addLegend(pal = pal, values = ~name, title = "name")

title: PS10: Geospatial Data
text:
2.1 Read and Manipulate Data
First, read in the data from countypres_2000-2020.csv and assign it to the variable pres_results. Then, filter the data using the year variable to only get the data for the 2020 election.

pres_results <- read_csv("countypres_2000-2020.csv")

Rows: 72617 Columns: 12
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (7): state, state_po, county_name, office, candidate, party, mode
dbl (5): year, county_fips, candidatevotes, totalvotes, version

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

# Filter for just the year 2020
pres_results_2020 <- pres_results |> filter(year == 2020)

Use head() to take a look at the dataset.

head(pres_results_2020)

# A tibble: 6 × 12
   year state   state_po county_name county_fips office       candidate    party
  <dbl> <chr>   <chr>    <chr>             <dbl> <chr>        <chr>        <chr>
1  2020 ALABAMA AL       AUTAUGA            1001 US PRESIDENT JOSEPH R BI… DEMO…
2  2020 ALABAMA AL       AUTAUGA            1001 US PRESIDENT OTHER        OTHER
3  2020 ALABAMA AL       AUTAUGA            1001 US PRESIDENT DONALD J TR… REPU…
4  2020 ALABAMA AL       BALDWIN            1003 US PRESIDENT JOSEPH R BI… DEMO…
5  2020 ALABAMA AL       BALDWIN            1003 US PRESIDENT OTHER        OTHER
6  2020 ALABAMA AL       BALDWIN            1003 US PRESIDENT DONALD J TR… REPU…
# ℹ 4 more variables: candidatevotes <dbl>, totalvotes <dbl>, version <dbl>,
#   mode <chr>


Currently, the data is presented at the county level, we need to compute the total number of votes for each candidate by state, and determine the winner party.

Group the data by state_po (The state postal code abbreviation: e.g. CA for California) and party,
use summarize() to sum the number of candidatevotes of all the counties,
use slice_max() to get only the state_po and party pairings that have the largest vote counts,
then select() the state_po and party columns.
Finally, use vote counts to find the winning party by state instead of by county. Assign this to the variable state_by_party.


state_by_party = pres_results_2020 |> group_by(state_po, party) |> summarize(total_votes = sum(candidatevotes)) |> group_by(state_po) |> slice_max(total_votes, n = 1) |> select(state_po, party)

`summarise()` has regrouped the output.
ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by state_po and party.
ℹ Output is grouped by state_po.
ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(state_po, party))` for per-operation grouping
  (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.


Use head() or slice_head() to check state_by_party.

head(state_by_party)

# A tibble: 6 × 2
# Groups:   state_po [6]
  state_po party     
  <chr>    <chr>     
1 AK       REPUBLICAN
2 AL       REPUBLICAN
3 AR       REPUBLICAN
4 AZ       DEMOCRAT  
5 CA       DEMOCRAT  
6 CO       DEMOCRAT

title: PS10: Geospatial Data
text:
2.2 Merging Data
Before we can use the dataset to make the election results map, we need to merge it with geospatial data from the usmap package. We can use an inner_join() on the output of us_map() and our state_by_party tibble.

us_states <- inner_join(us_map(), state_by_party, by = c("abbr" = "state_po"))

title: PS10: Geospatial Data
text:
2.3 Making Maps
The us_map() function gives us a simple feature collection that contains the geospatial data to graph the electoral votes map. To plot this map with ggplot() all you have to do is add a geom_sf() layer.

2.3.1 Basic Map
Let’s start with a basic graph of the choropleth map, plotting the tiles that correspond to each state. pass the us_states data to ggplot and add a geom_sf() layer

us_states |> ggplot() + geom_sf()










2.3.2 Color by Party
Because the simple features data has been merged with state_by_party, we also have the winner party of each state. Therefore, we can map this variable to the fill aesthetic to color-code the tiles according to party. To make the colors align with the typical colors of the Democratic and Republican parties, add a scale_fill_manual(values = c("#0077cc", "#cc2200")) layer as well.

us_states |> ggplot() + geom_sf(aes(fill = party)) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("#0077cc", "#cc2200"))










2.3.3 Label States
Add a geom_sf_text() layer with a mapping for label = abbr, which is the state name post office abbreviation. (You may want to mess with the size and color settings inside geom_sf_text in order for the labels to look nice on the colored backgrounds.)

us_states |> ggplot() + geom_sf(aes(fill = party)) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("#0077cc", "#cc2200")) + geom_sf_text(aes(label = abbr), size = 2, color = "white")










2.3.4 Final Step
Add the theme_void layer to leave just the map without the axis or gridlines. (Not required, but you may also want to try and position the legend below the map and remove the title.)

us_states |> ggplot() + geom_sf(aes(fill = party)) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("#0077cc", "#cc2200")) + geom_sf_text(aes(label = abbr), size = 2, color = "white") + theme_void()

title: PS10: Geospatial Data
text:
2.4 Interactive maps with ggiraph
We can make the map interactive by using functions from "ggiraph". In this case, the function geom_sf_interactive() allows us to display a tooltip feature when we hover over a particular state.

2.4.1 Basic Interactive Plot
Replace geom_sf() with geom_sf_interactive and add the mapping tooltip = party. Give this plot to the girafe function to make the interactive plot.

girafe(us_states |> ggplot() + geom_sf_interactive(aes(fill = party, tooltip=party)) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("#0077cc", "#cc2200")) + geom_sf_text(aes(label = abbr), size = 2, color = "white") + theme_void())







2.4.2 Final Interactive Plot
It would be better to have a more informative message displayed by the tooltip, for instance, to be able to display the name of the state, as well as the proportion of votes for each party. This requires more data manipulation so that we know the proportion of votes that each party obtained in each state.
The following pipeline gives us such a table:

state_by_party_prop = pres_results |> 
  filter(year == 2020) |> 
  group_by(state_po, party) |>
  summarize(votes = sum(candidatevotes)) |> 
  mutate(prop = round(100 * votes / sum(votes), 2)) |> 
  filter(party %in% c("DEMOCRAT", "REPUBLICAN")) |> 
  select(state_po, party, prop) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = party, values_from = prop) |> 
  mutate(winner = ifelse(DEMOCRAT - REPUBLICAN > 0, "DEM", "REP"))

`summarise()` has regrouped the output.
ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by state_po and party.
ℹ Output is grouped by state_po.
ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(state_po, party))` for per-operation grouping
  (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.

head(state_by_party_prop)

# A tibble: 6 × 4
# Groups:   state_po [6]
  state_po DEMOCRAT REPUBLICAN winner
  <chr>       <dbl>      <dbl> <chr> 
1 AK           43.0       53.1 REP   
2 AL           36.6       62.0 REP   
3 AR           34.8       62.4 REP   
4 AZ           49.4       49.1 DEM   
5 CA           63.5       34.3 DEM   
6 CO           55.4       41.9 DEM   


As we’ve done before, the next step involves merging the us_map() geospatial data with the state_by_party_prop information. Use the code from the static plot as a guide and perform an inner_join.

usp <- inner_join(us_map(), state_by_party_prop, by = c("abbr" = "state_po"))

We can remake the interactive plot specifying a tooltip feature that displays the full name of the State from the us_map() data, together with the percentage of votes for both parties. Set a tooltip similarly to the previous part, but now using tooltip = sprintf("%s\nDem: %s%%\nRep: %s%%", full, DEMOCRAT, REPUBLICAN).

girafe(usp |> ggplot() + geom_sf_interactive(aes(fill = winner, tooltip = sprintf("%s\nDem: %s%%\nRep: %s%%", full, DEMOCRAT, REPUBLICAN))) + scale_fill_manual(values = c("#0077cc", "#cc2200")) + geom_sf_text(aes(label = abbr), size = 2, color = "white") + theme_void())

title: Closeread Template
text:
This is a sample closeread document.




The narrative appears on this side bar (left margin).




The image on the right depicts three species of iris flowers and their corresponding Sepal and Petal elements.




We refer to this image element as a sticky element. Why? Because as you scroll down, the image of the iris flowers gets stuck in the same place.




When you trigger a sticky element, it will be displayed in the main section (to the right of this sidebar).




Here’s another sticky element. It’s a simple scatter plot based on the iris data set, which is a built-in data frame in R.




This narrative section is displayed as a continuous piece of text instead of being broken down into separated paragraphs.

There are 3 species of iris flowers:

- Setosa (red)
- Versicolor (green)
- Virginica (blue)




Here’s a third sticky element.




It’s just a table with summary statistics.




And finally a fourth sticky element.




A colorful plot using facets to show the distribution of Sepal Length among the three iris species.

























Species
avg_sepal_length
sd_sepal_length




setosa
5.006
0.3524897


versicolor
5.936
0.5161711


virginica
6.588
0.6358796

title: A Little About Me
text:
Thanks for coming by! I’m a Bay Area native currently studying Applied Mathematics and Data Science at UC Berkeley.
I enjoy working out (on a push/pull/legs split), hooping, computers (hardware/architecture), cars/motorsport (I’m a Vehicle Dynamics engineer for Berkeley Formula Racing), and EDM (I was a top 350 Spotify listener of William Black worldwide in 2025). I’m also pretty proud of my general knowledge base, which I’m constantly adding to.

title: Welcome!
text: Hi! I’m Steven Wang. I’m a UC Berkeley undergrad studying Applied Mathematics and Data Science. WIP–poke around!

title: 2024-25 NBA Salary Data
text:
Salary Distribution and Statistics




10 Highest Salaries




Among star players, we see multiple contracts paying $49.2M in 2024-25 as a result of the salary cap and the max contract value being limited to 35% of the cap for players with 10+ years of experience.




look at curry man so inspirational




10 Highest Team Payrolls




Bradley Beal is singlehandedly responsible for the Suns payroll. Book and KD have always earned their share; Beal pulled off one of the greatest contract heists of the decade.




Salary vs Experience




We see a clear salary limit in the first four years of careers. First-round picks are limited to fixed-scale 4-year rookie contracts, while later picks are typically only offered contracts at or near the minimum.




These restrictions are substantially lifted after the rookie contract expires, or from the 4th year onward, in the case of rookie-scale extensions.




Salary Market by Position




Teams tend to value great point guards more than great players in other positions, like steph.




Look at bron at the top of the sf market.
